perturbation.original_text: The term of the Project shall be from May 25, 2005 and shall continue at least until the desired drug delivery technology has been proven commercially viable, or until mutually terminated by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of the Project shall be from May 25, 2005 and shall continue at least until the Parties deem the project complete, or until terminated at Nanolution's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the termination clause gives Nanolution unilateral control, conflicting with the initial agreement for mutual termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, allowing Nanolution to unilaterally terminate, could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts governed by the UCC, as it gives one party excessive control and the potential to act opportunistically.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract allowed for termination by mutual agreement, ensuring both parties had a say. The changed text grants Nanolution sole discretion to terminate, removing the requirement for mutual agreement. This shift could allow Nanolution to act opportunistically, violating the obligation of good faith in the contract's performance and enforcement, as per UCC § 1-304: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.'

perturbation.original_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means all technical or business information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA that is identified at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter as being confidential and proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means any information, regardless of its nature or prior disclosure, that Nanolution designates as confidential at any time during or after the term of the JRA.
perturbation.explanation: This redefinition broadens the scope of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' and removes the requirement for timely identification. It attempts to retroactively classify information as confidential, regardless of prior disclosure or public availability. This creates an imbalance and uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification attempts to classify information as confidential retroactively, contradicting the definition of 'trade secret' under 18 U.S. Code § 1839. A trade secret must be kept confidential and have economic value from not being generally known. Designating information as confidential retroactively undermines the requirement that it be secret and have value from being secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' to include information designated as such at any time, even after disclosure, contradicting the traditional understanding of trade secrets which requires continuous confidentiality to maintain its status. The original text required timely identification. The LLM Law Explanation references 18 U.S. Code § 1839 which emphasizes the importance of secrecy and economic value derived from that secrecy for something to qualify as a trade secret. The scraped law snippet 2 is not helpful, but snippet 1 references Title 18, which is relevant here.

perturbation.original_text: 3. In consideration of each and every disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, the Parties agree to: (a) treat as confidential and to preserve the confidentiality of all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION; (b) use any and all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION solely in connection with the performance of the IRA and for no other purpose;
perturbation.changed_text: 3. In consideration of each and every disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, the Parties agree to: (a) treat as proprietary INFORMATION, unless publicly known or generally available; (b) use any and all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION solely at Nanolution's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The change in wording weakens the confidentiality obligation and grants Nanolution discretionary control over the use of confidential information. This deviates from the original intent of mutual protection of proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By giving Nanolution sole discretion, this weakens the contractual obligation of confidentiality. It could lead to a breach of contract claim if NaturalNano's Confidential Information is used for a purpose outside the JRA, contradicting established contract law principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: breach of contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you breach of contract A breach of contract occurs whenever a party who entered a contract fails to perform their promised obligations. Due to the frequency of breaches of contract, a robust body of law has grown to resolve the ensuing disputes. The overarching goal of contract law is to place the harmed party in the same economic position they would have been in had no breach of contract occurred. As a result, the default remedy available for a breach of contract is monetary damages . Generally, these damages are limited to what is listed in the contract and, unlike damages from tort cases, courts do not award punitive damages for breaches of contract. For example, if a party agrees to pay $50,000 to have their house painted but is only willing to hand over $10,000 once the painting is complete, the court will award the painters $40,000 in damages. This hesitancy to award punitive damages is due to the theory of efficient breach which argues that breaching contracts and paying damages is sometimes economically beneficial for society as a whole. Nonetheless, in specific circumstances, a party may successfully recover more money than initially contracted for under the doctrine of reliance damages . Under this doctrine , a party who reasonably relied upon a contract that was later breached can be granted compensation for the reasonable expenses they incurred due to that reliance. For example, a party who purchases lifeguard equipment in reliance upon a pool construction contract’s fulfillment may be able to recover the costs of the lifeguard equipment in the event of a breach. Reliance damages are based upon the principle of promissory estoppel , and granting them is subject to the court’s discretion. That said, parties harmed by a breach of contract have a duty to mitigate that harm. For example, before they could recover, the aforementioned lifeguard equipment buyer must first attempt to resell the equipment to a new buyer. Failure to satisfy the duty to mitigate will result in an inability to recover damages. In scenarios where damages are insufficient, a court may instead award specific performance . Under the specific performance remedy, the breaching party must attempt to fulfill the terms of the contract as best as possible. Specific performance, however, is generally only awarded when dealing with one-of-a-kind assets like real estate . Parties wishing to contract around the above remedies can do so through the use of liquidated damages provisions. These provisions establish in advance how much money a breaching party must pay and sidestep the expensive and time-consuming process of determining the actual damage caused by the breach. While liquidated damages clauses are generally allowed, a court may strike one down if the clause appears to be proxying for punitive damages or if the terms of the clause are unconscionable . [Last reviewed in June of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics wex articles business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure legal education and practice legal theory money and financial problems Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts established contract law principles concerning confidentiality. The original text emphasizes the preservation of confidentiality, while the changed text weakens this obligation by treating confidential information as 'proprietary INFORMATION, unless publicly known or generally available' and granting Nanolution sole discretion in its use. This misalignment weakens the confidentiality obligation and grants Nanolution discretionary control over the use of confidential information which deviates from the original intent of mutual protection of proprietary information. The law snippet defines 'breach of contract' as a failure to perform promised obligations, and the original contract promises confidentiality. By giving one party sole discretion, the modified contract introduces the possibility of a breach, if 'Confidential Information is used for a purpose outside the JRA, contradicting established contract law principles,' as stated in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The confidentiality obligations outlined in this agreement will persist indefinitely, unless Nanolution provides written notice of release.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the time limit on confidentiality and makes it subject to Nanolution's discretion, contradicting the original intent of a limited confidentiality period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations - Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While this isn't a direct contradiction of the statute of limitations, removing the time limitation makes potential breaches far into the future actionable indefinitely, which is generally disfavored. New York CPLR 213 sets the statute of limitations for breach of contract at six years. This modification attempts to circumvent this.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The original contract language limits the confidentiality obligations to five years from the termination of the JRA. The changed text removes this limitation and makes the confidentiality obligations indefinite, subject only to Nanolution's discretion. This contradicts the general legal principle, as explained by the LLM and supported by NY CPLR § 213, which sets a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract. By making the confidentiality obligations indefinite, the changed text attempts to circumvent this statute of limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both mention "SECTION 213 Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property". The changed text could expose a party to liability far beyond the statutory period, which is disfavored under the law.
